elements.sent-2411-001: Motivated by knot theory, it is natural to define the orientation-reversal of a quandle orbit by inverting all the translations given by elements of that orbit.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this short note we observe that this natural notion is unsuited to medial quandles.
elements.sent-2411-003: A quandle is defined by a binary operation $\triangleright$ on a set $Q$, which satisfies these three axioms.
elements.sent-2411-004: Special cases of this definition were considered almost 100 years ago, but the modern theory dates back to the introduction of quandles in classical knot theory by Joyce \cite{J and Matveev \cite{M in the 1980s.}}
elements.sent-2411-005: The central object of classical knot theory is a link, i.e., a collection of finitely many simple closed curves in $\mathbb R ^3$; the curves are oriented, pairwise disjoint, and piecewise smooth.
elements.sent-2411-006: Each individual curve is a component of the link.
elements.sent-2411-007: Links are represented by diagrams, and the fundamental quandle of a link is generated by the arcs of a diagram.
elements.sent-2411-008: The operation $\triangleright$ of this quandle may be interpreted as ``passing under, from right to left.'' See Figure \ref{fig1 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Recall that a quandle variety $\mathcal V$ is the collection of quandles that satisfy some particular set of formulas involving $\triangleright$ and $\triangleright^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-010: Two well-known examples are the variety $\M$ of medial quandles, defined by $(w \triangleright x) \triangleright (y \triangleright z)= (w \triangleright y) \triangleright (x \triangleright z) \thickspace \forall w,x,y,z \in Q$, and the variety $\V_n$ of $n$-quandles, defined by $\beta_y^n(x) =x \thickspace \forall x,y \in Q$.
elements.sent-2411-011: Note that some authors, like Joyce \cite{J , call medial quandles ``abelian.'' If $Q$ is an arbitrary quandle and $\mathcal V$ is a quandle variety, then $Q$ gives rise to an element $Q_{\mathcal V \in \mathcal V$ in a natural way: The formulas that define $\mathcal V$ yield an equivalence relation on $Q$, and $Q_{\mathcal V}$ is the quotient of $Q$ modulo this equivalence relation.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In particular, Joyce \cite[Sec.\ 10]{J refers to $Q_{\M}$ as the ``largest abelian quotient of $Q$.'' A basic operation of knot theory is that we may reverse the orientation of one of the components of a link.}
elements.sent-2411-013: As is clear from Figure \ref{fig1 , the effect of this operation on the link quandle is to interchange the functions $\beta_a$ and $\beta_a^{-1}$ for each arc $a$ that belongs to the reversed component.}
elements.sent-2411-014: If $Q$ is a quandle and $x \in Q$ then $Q_x$ denotes the orbit of $x$ in $Q$, i.e., the smallest subset of $Q$ that contains $x$ and is closed under $\beta_y$ and $\beta_y^{-1}$ for every $y \in Q$.
elements.sent-2411-015: It is not hard to see that in a link quandle, the diagram arcs corresponding to a single component all belong to the same orbit.
elements.sent-2411-016: If that component's orientation is reversed, then every one of these arcs has its orientation reversed.
elements.sent-2411-018: Let $Q$ be a quandle with an orbit $Q_x$.
elements.sent-2411-019: Then the quandle obtained from $Q$ by reversing the orientation of $Q_x$ is the quandle $\Qr(x)$ with the same underlying set as $Q$, in which $\br_y=\beta_y\thickspace \allowbreak \forall y \notin Q_x$ and $\br_y=\beta_y ^{-1 \thickspace \allowbreak \forall y \in Q_x$.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Verifying that Definition \ref{orrev really does yield a quandle $\Qr(x)$ is not difficult; we leave the details to the reader.}
elements.sent-2411-021: We should say that although we have not seen Definition \ref{orrev in the literature, we presume that such an easy and intuitive idea has occurred to other researchers.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our purpose in writing this note is to bring up the problem of defining a notion of orientation-reversal that is appropriate for quandles in a variety $\V$.
elements.sent-2411-023: For $n$-quandles there is no difficulty: $\beta_y^n$ is the identity map if and only if $\beta_y^{-n}$ is the identity map, so $Q$ is an $n$-quandle if and only if $\Qr(x)$ is an $n$-quandle.
elements.sent-2411-024: But for other varieties, there is no reason to expect the defining formulas to be satisfied when some $\beta$ maps are replaced by their inverses.
elements.sent-2411-025: For such a variety, the natural way to define orientation-reversal is to consider the quotient $\Qr(x)_{\V}$, which may be a smaller quandle than $\Qr(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Observe that if we reverse the orientation of the same orbit twice, the result is the original quandle: $(\Qr(x))^{rev (x)=Q$.}
elements.sent-2411-028: This observation motivates our next definition.
elements.sent-2411-029: A quandle variety $\V$ is suited for orientation-reversals if it has the property that for every $Q \in \V$, $\Qr(x)_{\mathcal{V }$ determines $Q$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In the remainder of the note we show that $\M$ is not suited for orientation-reversals, by providing an example of an infinite medial quandle $Q$ with the property that $\Qr(x)_{\M}$ is a trivial quandle with only two elements.
elements.sent-2411-031: Let $\Lambda = \mathbb Z[t,t^{-1 ]$ be the ring of Laurent polynomials in the variable $t$, with integer coefficients.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Let $J$ be the principal ideal of $\Lambda$ generated by $t^2+t-1$, and let $N=\Lambda/J$.
elements.sent-2411-033: Notice that if $x\in N$ then $(1-t)x=(1-t)x+(t^2+t-1)x=t^2 x$ and hence $x=(t^2+t)x$.
elements.sent-2411-034: We leave it to the reader to use these properties to prove the following.
elements.sent-2411-035: Every element of $N$ is $x=n_1t+n_2t^2+J$ for some unique $n_1,n_2 \in \mathbb Z$.
elements.sent-2411-036: In the terminology of Jedli\v{c ka et al. \cite{JPZ , our example $Q$ is a free $(t^2+t-1)$-quandle on a two-element set.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: It is described explicitly as follows.
elements.sent-2411-038: Let $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ be two disjoint copies of $N$.
elements.sent-2411-039: If $x \in N$, we use $x_i$ to denote the copy of $x$ in $Q_i$.
elements.sent-2411-041: We build a medial quandle $Q$ on $Q_1 \cup Q_2$ by defining \begin{equation}\beta_{y_j}(x_i)=x_i \triangleright y_j = (m_j-m_i+tx + (1-t)y)_i \in Q_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-042: Elementary arguments verify that $Q$ is a medial quandle, and \begin{equation}\beta_{y_j}^{-1}(x_i)=t^{-1} \cdot (m_i-m_j+x - (1-t)y)_i \in Q_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: If $x \in N$ then \begin{equation}0_1 \triangleright (t^{-2}(x-1))_2 = (1+t \cdot 0+(1-t)(t^{-2}(x-1)))_1\end{equation} \begin{equation}= (1+t \cdot 0+t^2(t^{-2}(x-1)))_1 =(1+x-1)_1=x_1 ,\end{equation} so $x_1$ is in the same orbit of $Q$ as $0_1$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Notice that for convenience, in equations we sometimes suppress the coset notation for an element of $N$.
elements.sent-2411-047: For instance, in the last line of the proof of Lemma \ref{orbits we write $(-1+x+1)_2$ rather than $(-1+J+x+1+J)_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Using the formulas for $\beta_{y_j}$ and $\beta^{-1 _{y_j}$ displayed above, in $\Qr(0_2)$ we have \begin{equation}(w_1 \triangleright x_1) \triangleright { } (y_1 \triangleright z_2) = (tw+(1-t)x)_1 \triangleright (t^{-1} \cdot (m_1-m_2+y-(1-t)z))_1 =(t(tw+(1-t)x)+(1-t)(t^{-1} \cdot (-1+y-(1-t)z)))_1 =(t^2w+t(1-t)x+t^2(t^{-1} \cdot (-1+y-t^2 z)))_1 =(t^2w+t^3x+t(-1+y-t^2 z))_1=(t^2w+t^3x-t+ty-t^3 z)_1.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-050: In $\Qr(0_2)_{\M}$ we have $(w_1 \triangleright x_1) \triangleright (y_1 \triangleright z_2)=(w_1 \triangleright y_1) \triangleright (x_1 \triangleright z_2)$, and therefore \begin{equation}\label{require} (t^2w+t^3x-t+ty-t^3 z)_1=(t^2w+t^3y-t+tx-t^3 z)_1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-053: As $t^2+t^3-t=t(t^2+t-1) \in J$, it follows that $a_1 = (t^2+a)_1$ in $\Qr(0_2)_{\M}$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Using the facts that $a_1 = (t^2+a)_1$ and $a_1=(a-1)_1$ in $\Qr(0_2)_{\M}$ for all $a \in N$, we conclude that also $a_1=(a+t^2+t-1)_1 = (a+t-1)_1=(a+t)_1$ in $\Qr(0_2)_{\M}$ for all $a \in N$.
elements.sent-2411-057: According to Lemma \ref{element , the equalities $a_1=(a+t^2)_1$ and $a_1=(a+t)_1$ imply that $a_1=0_1$ in $\Qr(0_2)_{\M}$ for all $a \in N$.}
elements.sent-2411-058: That is, the orbit of $0_1$ in $\Qr(0_2)_{\M}$ has only one element.
elements.sent-2411-059: The proof of Lemma \ref{orbits shows that every element of the orbit $Q_{0_2}$ is $0_2 \triangleright y_1$ for some element $y_1$ of the orbit $Q_{0_1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The functions $\beta_{y_1}$ are the same in $Q$ and $\Qr(0_2)$, so it is also true that every element of $\Qr(0_2)_{0_2}$ is $0_2 \triangleright y_1$ for some $y_1 \in \Qr(0_2)_{0_1}$.
elements.sent-2411-061: Of course this property is inherited by the quotient quandle $\Qr(0_2)_{\M}$.
elements.sent-2411-062: But in the previous paragraph we showed that $(\Qr(0_2)_{\M ) _{0_1}$ has only one element.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Therefore $(\Qr(0_2)_{\M ) _{0_2}$ also has only one element.}
